Turns out heartbeat_timeout is triggered at 5s, not at the same time as the "connection". Given how in flux the timeout interface is, exposing just the time since the last one is the clearest, simplest and most user flexible option. 

> On 16 Nov 2015, at 12:46 pm, Hamish Willee <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
> IMO the only connection API we should supply is vehicle.last_heartbeat. This should have a value of None before a connection is established and we're getting heartbeats. After that it should be time since we got the last heartbeat (or possibly time since we got "message of any kind" from the vehicle).
> 
> My reasoning is that any other information implies that we really have a connection (ie are receiving and sending messages). In fact the only thing we KNOW is the freshness of the last message. I want this in people's minds. I don't see adding a boolean they can check on as making this any simpler than just defining a callback on the time value.
> 
> We will still need something to perform the function of vehicle.connected but it really needs to imply "valid vehicle" rather than connection state (ie solely for vehicle.close() and as a backup if the user does not connection monitoring.. I would also propose that we use it to (after removing any callbacks) cause other function calls on the object to raise an exception . That will encourage the developer to immediately set the value to None themselves (a familiar pattern for those who learned C++ first :-0 ). If we do it this way probably no need to have an API to set this timeout time.
> 
> Irrespective of whether we have the additional boolean for tracking a timeout we still need to think about what advice we give people for how they take action on it/set the value.
> 
> Any ideas?
> 
> My thoughts ...
> 
> The vehicle.last_heartbeat value is a measure of the "freshness" of information from the vehicle. Scripts which rely on timely position information may wish to modify their behaviour based on this value (for example when tracking a moving target).
> 
> Higher values of this attribute (>5 seconds, say) may indicate that the link is broken (in which case developers may wish to pause or restart the script).
> 
> Note: The connection to the vehicle is potentially "lossy": Irrespective of the last_heartbeat value you cannot assume that messages are (or are not) getting to the vehicle.
> 
> ... then having an example that perhaps just exits the script following a 6 second timeout?
>

As a proof of concept, I modified the `docs/text/introduction.rst` as follows:

```
Introduction
============

.. autosummary::
    :toctree: generated

    tsfresh.feature_extraction.feature_calculators


Why do you need such a module?
```

Do integrate this in the "normal" sphinx-build (done via `python setup.py shinx-build`), simply add

```
# Autogenerate some content
autosummary_generate = True
```

to the `docs.conf.py` file.

```
[Sat Jan 23 14:08:53.308033 2016] [proxy:debug] [pid 25638] proxy_util.c(2145): AH00942: HTTP: has acquired connection for (localhost)
[Sat Jan 23 14:08:53.308046 2016] [proxy:debug] [pid 25638] proxy_util.c(2199): [client 73.202.84.81:38361] AH00944: connecting http://localhost/.well-known/acme-challenge to localhost:80

[Sat Jan 23 14:08:53.308298 2016] [proxy:debug] [pid 25638] proxy_util.c(2408): [client 73.202.84.81:38361] AH00947: connected /.well-known/acme-challenge to httpd-UDS:0
[Sat Jan 23 14:08:53.308559 2016] [proxy:error] [pid 25638] (2)No such file or directory: AH02454: HTTP: attempt to connect to Unix domain socket /var/run/caldavd_requests/unsecured.sock (localhost) failed
```

I think the clue is "http: has determined UDS as /var/run/**caldavd**_requests/unsecured.sock". caldavd is associated with the calendar & contacts service. For some reason, this request is being routed by the reverse proxy (aka 'service proxy) to the unix domain socket (UDS) belonging to the calendar & contacts service, which in my case is disabled. The reverse proxy backend connection fails, causing the client's request to the proxy to fail with 503:

```
<server> "<server>" <ip> - - [23/Jan/2016:14:18:48 -0800] "GET /.well-known/acme-challenge HTTP/1.1" 503 1585 "-" "Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10_11_3) AppleWebKit/601.4.4 (KHTML, like Gecko) Version/9.0.3 Safari/601.4.4"
```

The custom error page /customerror/websitesoff403.html is used for this failure, even if your websites are actually enabled. Perhaps the config logic assumes that if a backend connection fails, it's because the backend service is disabled.

Ultimately I think the problem is whatever config is causing the ".well-known" request to be routed to the calendar & contacts backend instead of one of the apache2 sites configured in /Library/Server/Web/Config/apache2/sites

```
import requests_toolbelt.adapters.appengine
requests_toolbelt.adapters.appengine.monkeypatch()
import pkg_resources
old_get_distribution = pkg_resources.get_distribution

def mp_get_distribution(*args, **kwargs):
	# print args
	# print kwargs
	try:
		res = old_get_distribution(*args, **kwargs)
	except:
		class Mock(object):
			pass

		mock = Mock()
		mock.version = '1.9.0'
		return mock
	
	return res

pkg_resources.get_distribution = mp_get_distribution

from google.cloud import storage
```

OK, I've narrowed this down to strange behavior between Python 2's raw_input() function and pygtk.

cmd2 imports the pyperclip module, which imports gtk if it exists on the system. I'm guessing that your Ubuntu system does not have Python gtk installed, so pyperclip  is using a fallback method for its clipboard functionality.

For some reason, if gtk is imported, then no printing will occur when raw_input() is blocking. I do have a work around for you. After calling Cmd's initializer, set its **use_rawinput member** to False. This tells cmd2 to use readline directly to get its input.

```
Cmd.__init__(self, use_ipython=False)
self.use_rawinput = False
```

Below is code that demonstrates the bug without involving cmd2. As written the code will print correctly. If you uncomment the "import gtk" line then the printing will not work.

```
#!/usr/bin.env python
# coding=utf-8

#import gtk
import six.moves as sm
import threading
import time

stop_printing = False


def print_thread_worker():
        time_slept = 0
        SLEEP_INTERVAL = 0.2

        while not stop_printing:
            time.sleep(SLEEP_INTERVAL)
            time_slept += SLEEP_INTERVAL

            if time_slept >= 1:
                print("PRINTING")
                time_slept = 0

if __name__ == '__main__':

    print("Wait a second for the printing to start and then hit Enter to exit")

    print_thread = threading.Thread(target=print_thread_worker)
    print_thread.start()

    try:
        line = sm.input('cli> ')
    except (Exception, KeyboardInterrupt):
        pass

    stop_printing = True
    if print_thread.is_alive():
        print_thread.join()
```

I have no idea the root cause of this. For now, if you plan on using Python 2 with a system that has pygtk, then use the workaround I described.

To provide a concrete example to @minrk's instructions for anyone struggling with this like I was.

IPython searches for kernel specifications in one of three spots:

Unix:
- `/usr/share/jupyter/kernels`
- `/usr/local/share/jupyter/kernels`
- `~/.ipython/kernels`

Windows:
- `%PROGRAMDATA%\jupyter\kernels`

A kernel spec is nothing more than a folder with a `kernel.json` file that specifies how to launch that kernel. If I wanted to use a specific IPython profile (named `my-profile`), I could do something like:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.ipython/kernels/my-custom-kernel
cat > ~/.ipython/kernels/my-custom-kernel/kernel.json <<EOF
{
    "display_name": "My Custom Python Kernel",
    "language": "python",
    "argv": [
        "python",
        "-m", "ipykernel",
        "--profile=my-profile",
        "-f", "{connection_file}"
    ],
    "env": {
    }
}
EOF
```

When launching a Jupyter notebook server, you'll now have the choice of launching either a `Python 2` kernel or a `My Custom Python Kernel` kernel.

[`@ellisonbg` commented](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/805#issuecomment-3360457): There are two different issues here: one is the idea of grouping, the other the idea of nesting.  Fernando and I have talked extensively about the fact that the way Mathematica handles grouping through nesting is quite annoying if you use it for anything non-trivial.  Our idea is to implement grouping, but not nesting.  How would this work?

We would keep the notebook as a linear sequence of cells.  We would introduce different levels of heading cells, but these would be flat/inline (like latex section/subsection/subsubsection) rather than nested like the Mathematica notebook.  We feel this style of document will be easier to import/export to different formats and is much simpler for users to manage and for us to develop a UI for.  To introduce grouping like functionality, we thought about using the locations of different heading levels to identify logical groups that could be collapsed, moved and treated as a unit.  We feel this would give the desired benefits of grouping without the complexity of nesting.  The first step along with path is to create the heading cells, them we can start to think about a UI for using them as logical groups.

It works! `curl -L https://raw.githubusercontent.com/1138-4EB/vunit/issue-runner/issue_runner/issue_runner.sh | sh -s 337` will:

- Create a subdir named `issue-337`.
- Save `run.sh`, `tb_repro.vhd` and `tb_mwe.vhd.txt` from the first message of the issue to separate files.
- Execute `run.sh`.

Requirements:

- GitHub does not allow uploading `.vhd` files. So, `.txt` must be appended.
- The script parses the body to search for `#>> filename.ext` lines. All the content between such a token and the next one is saved to the file. Therefore, a las dummy token, `#>> anything` must be added.

Provided feature:

Users can put as many files as desired in the first message of the issue. Any maintainer/collaborator can test provided (non)working example in any machine with docker. It can be used in [play-with-docker.com](https://labs.play-with-docker.com/).

The single limitation is that the docker image that is used in `run.sh` must be available. There is a workaround for issues requiring tools that can not be public: edit `issue_runner.sh` to add a sed command before the execution of `run.sh`.

The sources are just a couple of short cripts: https://github.com/1138-4EB/vunit/tree/issue-runner/issue_runner

Apologies if this is the wrong place, but is there a way to have boto3 respect the default MIME type of a file, instead of this behavior:
> If you do not provide anything for ContentType to ExtraArgs, the end content type will always be binary/octet-stream.

Here's a couple of ways to do this:
Preferably you would use [shared configuration](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/configuration.html#shared-credentials-file) which will automatically load the credentials based on the `default` profile or the specified profile name passed to the `Session` or `Client`. This includes [assuming a role](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/configuration.html#assume-role-provider) and will handle what your function does automatically. Something like:
```
# In ~/.aws/credentials:
[development]
aws_access_key_id=foo
aws_access_key_id=bar

# In ~/.aws/config
[profile crossaccount]
role_arn=arn:aws:iam:...
source_profile=development
```
Then just pass `profile_name=crossaccount` to your `Client` or `Session` and boto will sort out assuming a role for you.

Or update your assume role code to apply the credentials to a [session](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/core/session.html) and then use that session to create clients. Though hard coding your credentials in your source code is definitely not recommended.

I like this plan, and it warrants some more exploration.  

Firstly, It might be a bit odd to use this approach in only one place.  I think we should consider what we might gain from using the `FieldValidator` approach on a few more types.  For example:
- `StringType.regex`:  I can't think of anything too great to add here.  Maybe a human-readable description of the regex.  It also gives us a good place to cache the compiled regex, if we want to add that feature.
- `NumberType/DecimalType.min_value`, `NumberType/DecimalType.max_value`:  could add `inclusive` boolean, for controlling `>` vs `>=`.  also allows code reuse between `NumberType` and `DecimalType`.

The problem here is that I don't think we can reasonably make each of these validators behave like the data type they proxy.  i.e. I doubt we want to do this:

```python
class Regex(FieldValidator, str):
    ...
```

Next up, the placement of the messages in the class `MESSAGES` attribute complicates things when passing a `Choices` object.  For example, this is not so pretty:

```python
class MyModel(Model):
    foo = StringType(
        choices=Choices([1, 2, 3], message=BaseType.messages['choices']))
```

It makes sense for the canonical location for the choices message to be on the `Choices` validator, which would make passing in the message unnecessary, but I'm not sure if that's too big of a breaking change to make.

I believe you are talking about the logic regarding where Python bindings should be installed. I made it a requirement that the location of the python bindings live under the prefix. This is required for the install  and staged installs (using DESTDIR) to work correctly under Linux/FreeBSD.

I had a quick google and looks like the site-packages directory lives under ``/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages`` under OSX which is a bit of problem as that obviously isn't underneath ``/usr/local``. However it looks like if you use a homebrew version of Python you will find it at ``/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-package`` and for mac ports it will be at ``/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/2.7/site-packages``.

On your mac where is the site-packages folder? What do you think the behaviour should be of Z3's build system when the python ``site-packages`` directory is not under the install prefix? It looks like things are okay for a homebrew install of python (it's under ``/usr/local``) but it looks like its a problem for other Python installs on OSX.

One approach would be to change the logic so that doesn't assume that the Python install lives under the install prefix and will just do an install to ``$(DESTDIR)/path/to/site-packages`` is but emit some sort of warning when this happens. I really don't like this though, the fact this would install something that isn't under the install prefix is **very misleading** and gross.

Perhaps we could when picking the ``site-packages`` directory, iterate through the contents of ``sys.path`` until we find one under the install prefix and give up if there isn't one.

What do you think?

To add another element, this isn't a great experience when using a template from the GitHub quick start repo or a template shared with you. You may not know what parameters to pass in until you try to deploy, and then have to work through each successive failure - the CLI 2.0 is only prompting on the first parameter it sees as missing, not providing a list of all the parameters it's going to need from the template.

With the CLI 1.0, when you deploy from template and there are parameters values you need to provide, the CLI will prompt for those if you don't specify them. With the CLI 2.0, you have to load the json template, see what parameters are being requested, and then pass a big long json string in under `--parameters` or take the additional step to create a parameters file and then pass that in. That's not a great experience.

So here's a working sample from one of our docs with CLI 1.0:

`azure group create --name myResourceGroup --location "West US" --template-uri https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure/azure-quickstart-templates/master/docker-simple-on-ubuntu/azuredeploy.json`

And then what I need to update the doc to with CLI 2.0:

`az group create --name myResourceGroup --location westus`

`az group deployment create --resource-group myResourceGroup --parameters '{"newStorageAccountName": {"value": "mystorageaccount"},"adminUsername": {"value": "azureuser"},"adminPassword": {"value": "P@ssw0rd!"},"dnsNameForPublicIP": {"value": "mypublicdns"}}' --template-uri https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure/azure-quickstart-templates/master/docker-simple-on-ubuntu/azuredeploy.json`

The CLI 2.0 implementation is not as user-friendly or intuitive. That's not an easy block to copy and paste and have a successful experience. Add in the differences in escaping and that's not cool.

Suggestion would be to provide interactive prompt for the required values or at the least to parse the provided template and provide a list of all the parameters the user needs to pass in and indicate what the user needs to add to the command.

Hi @lmazuel @mastermanu,

My issue is now fixed thanks to you two.
It was indeed the SAS token : 

```
container_permissions = ContainerPermissions.WRITE
blob_permissions = BlobPermissions.CREATE + BlobPermissions.WRITE + BlobPermissions.READ + BlobPermissions.DELETE

container_token = sas.generate_container(
            container_name,
            container_permissions + blob_permissions,
            datetime.now() + timedelta(hours=1)
        )

blob_filename = "backup-{}".format(datetime.now().strftime("%Y%m%d"))
url = blob_service.make_blob_url(container_name, blob_filename, sas_token=container_token)

client.iot_hub_resource.export_devices(rg, iothub.name, url, True)
```

All is working great now, thank you for the help.

E.g. https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/issues/218

I realized there is one obscure flaw here: because it abandons the task, then it could escape catch-all exception handlers. For example:
```python
async def start_in_trio():
    await trio.run_in_worker_thread(then_in_worker_thread, trio.current_await_in_trio_thread())

def then_in_worker_thread(await_in_trio_thread):
    await_in_trio_thread(back_in_trio)

async def back_in_trio():
    await asyncio.sleep(1)

trio.run(start_in_trio)
```
What this ought to do is propagate an error back through the worker thread to the starting function and then out of `trio.run`. What will actually happen with the current implementation is that the secret system task that `await_in_trio_thread` uses to call `back_in_trio_thread` will be unceremoniously abandoned, and raise an error inside the system task nursery, which will then cancel everything and raise a `TrioInternalError`.

This is tricky to avoid though, because in general we don't *know* how to resume a task that just yielded some foreign object. We could try doing a `coro.throw` but (a) that's awkward to arrange because we never do that otherwise, and (b) it doesn't necessarily work – twisted `Deferred`s don't have a `throw` method (!), and in principle the receiver could do anything with the error (though in practice it looks like asyncio, Tornado, Curio all let the error propagate like we'd want).

Some options:

Make sure that when we're running arbitrary user code and trying to capture the result, we always isolate the code into a standalone `Task`. Seems pretty overkill for this weird corner case? It looks like `await_in_trio_thread` is currently the only place like this in trio though (checked by grepping for `acapture`).

Add the ability to force a call to `throw`: not *too* bad, but if there's no `throw` method then we're back where we started.

Come up with some way to let the `TypeError` propagate properly instead of being converted to `TrioInternalError`?

Okay, managed to reproduce locally. Some tricky things required to get a trace: make the sigint trace print to stderr, b/c if it runs re-entrant with one of the stdout prints then you have two calls to the same `BufferedWriter.write` at the same time and it blows up; run *without* pytest's `-s` option b/c console printing seems to mess up timing, but then hack the `raise KeyboardInterrupt` to be `raise KeyError` so that pytest will actually print the captured stdout/stderr. Anyway:

```
---------------------------- Captured stdout call -----------------------------
sleeping @ 1491898063.0975676
woke up @ 1491898063.0975676
draining wakeup socket @ 1491898063.0975676
done draining
sleeping @ 1491898063.0975676
woke up @ 1491898063.0975676
draining wakeup socket @ 1491898063.0975676
done draining
sleeping @ 1491898063.0975676
sleeping @ 1491898063.0975676
woke up @ 1491898064.112795
wakeup sock is readable
PyErr_CheckSignals returned 0
draining wakeup socket @ 1491898064.112795
drained b'\x02'
done draining
sleeping @ 1491898064.112795
woke up @ 1491898083.113062
draining wakeup socket @ 1491898083.113062
done draining
finally @ 1491898083.113062
sleeping @ 1491898083.113062
woke up @ 1491898083.113062
draining wakeup socket @ 1491898083.113062
done draining
draining wakeup socket @ 1491898083.113062
drained b'\x00'
done draining
sleeping @ 1491898083.113062
woke up @ 1491898083.113062
draining wakeup socket @ 1491898083.113062
done draining
sleeping @ 1491898083.113062
woke up @ 1491898083.113062
draining wakeup socket @ 1491898083.113062
done draining
---------------------------- Captured stderr call -----------------------------
sigint handler @ 1491898083.113062
```

So the smoking gun here is:
* wake up at 1491898064.112795
* wakeup socket is readable, and we'll see contains `\x02`, so the C-level signal handler's wakeup fd code ran
* but `PyErr_CheckSignals` (called via `ctypes.pythonapi.PyErr_CheckSignals`) says that there are no signals pending and does not run the Python-level signal handler! The bytecode eval loop doesn't run the signal handler either!
* so we go back to sleep at 1491898064.112795
* then ~20 seconds later at 1491898083.113062, we wake up b/c the `select` timer expired
* and now the Python handler runs immediately (1491898083.113062)

The problem seems to be that in CPython's implementation of [`trip_signal`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/6fab78e9027f9ebd6414995580781b480433e595/Modules/signalmodule.c#L238-L291), the logic goes:
* set a flag saying that the signal arrived
* then write to the wakeup fd (good, we dodged a race condition! ...but wait!)
* **then** set a global flag saying "some signal arrived" and call `Py_AddPendingCall` (the latter of which is what sets the magic flag that tells the bytecode eval loop to immediately pause and run signal handlers)

Normatively, it should never be possible to get a write to the wakeup fd but *not* have a pending signal. So the fact that we observed this in the trace above seems to me to be a smoking gun: the problem is that the IO thread got scheduled to run after the write to the wakeup fd, but before `Py_AddPendingCall`.

So it's difficult to validate this because I can only reproduce the bug on Windows and I don't have a way to build CPython on Windows, but I'm ~99% confident that this is happening because `trip_signal` does things in the wrong order. It should write to the wakeup fd last, after it's set all the global variables.

I've also added code to my fork that adds new functionality to the api.rooms.get() method to allow for finding a room based on it's title.  I had meant to do this in a separate PR, but wasn't thinking as I started working on the code... so now this is a 2 for 1 PR... 

For this new feature, I find that I'm often needing to "get" a room based on the title, and don't know the roomId initially.  Currently you can do this with the code by running this function each time:

```
roomTitle = "My Room"
for r in api.rooms.list():
    if r.title == roomTitle:
        room = r
```

Which works but is tedious.  I've updated the room.get() method so that this works now: 

```
roomTitle = "My Room"
room = api.rooms.get(title=roomTitle)
```

If a single room is found with the title, it is returned.  If more than one room exists with the given title, a list of Rooms is returned.  If No room is round with the title, I'm currently returning a Room object with no details, but that behavior could be changed easily if there is another standard you are using for that case.

One quick thought: having more shards than our total capacity would likely be totally fine: additional shards would wait in queue to run until a slot had freed up. Over-sharding might also improve the feedback latency, because shorter running shards would go red earlier. The primary downside could be increased overhead, if each shard has additional storage requirements, or there is boilerplate due to creating more shards in `.travis.yml`.

To me, the compromise sounds like it would be the sanest first step. Internally for buildcache, we do something similar where we only write artifacts to the cache from a CI job and read from everywhere else (laptops, CI). This ensures repeatability and then abstracts us from the issues of different configurations/environments or just unreliability in caching results of intermediate work being performed. So its comes into play only if you are building/running code thats already in master.

Having said that, this sounds like it would provide little to no benefit while iterating on tests and would only benefit me in CI or when I am running tests locally (with no changes). A thought as a next step, what if we looked at invalidating just the target roots when there is a change so
`set(all_vts) - set(invalid_vts) = set(valid_vts)` and then the `set(valid_vts)` would be read from the cache? This might be similar to what you suggest in the first bullet point but instead of merging, demarcating the output from each target root so that they can be easily compose-able for the subsequent runs.

But you may be able to force a different coverage version by sneaking it in
to the requirements via ----unittest2-requirements (I'm assuming you're
using a python version that has a current unittest already), e.g.:

--pytest-cov-requirements="pytest-cov>=2.4,<2.5"
--unittest2-requirements="coverage>=1.0,<2.0"

or:

[pytest]
requirements: "pytest>=3.0.7,<4.0"
unittest2_requirements: "coverage>=1.0,<2.0"

Converting from coroutine to non-coroutine and back is painful process.
Making the method a coroutine prevent the pain if in future we will decide to do more work on spawning.

Also changing a method signature after publishing is impossible -- it breaks all existing code.
But I suggest adding a new synchronous method like `sync_spawn()`. Documentation should explicitly encourage using of `spawn()` from coroutines and call `async_spawn()` from synchronous code only.

If you like the idea -- please make a Pull Request.
Sure

I also agree to have "some" high-level wrappers around the API results — especially I observed an embrassing case: the `DockerContainer`'s attribute read in the result of `list` API and those read after calling `.show()` method *significantly differ* (because currently `DockerContainer` is a mere proxy of the API's return dictionaries and Docker's return values are not consistent unfortunately).

I think it's the way to go: wrapping API results with consistent interfaces.
For compatibility it would be nice to experiment on a separate fork and later we could provide an optional accessor to the underlying low-level API return values.

We actually do bundle Cap'n Proto binaries with nupic.core but the pycapnp library doesn't pick them up and instead downloads and installs a separate version. This should be less error-prone since we use custom flags and specify that everything is statically linked, etc and none of that is needed for the pycapnp version. Plus it is simpler to just include pycapnp in the requirements.txt rather than having to do a separate installation with args specifying where the capnproto binaries are.

So when we see errors during pycapnp installation, I suggest that we ask people to file issues on the pycapnp project: https://github.com/jparyani/pycapnp

Here is an _initial_ dictionary for schemas where the first column would be the new standardized field and the second column the actual field names. 

verbosity -> spVerbosity, tpVerbosity, etc (except when is necessary avoid conflicts)
random -> rng, rgen, etc
synapse -> syn
col -> columns (except when represent a very known computational term like the "col" of a table)
numColumns -> numberOfCols, numCols, etc
numCellsPerCol -> cellsPerCol
maxSegmentsPerCell -> maxSegsPerCell
segmentActivationThreshold -> activationThreshold
segmentMinThreshold -> minThreshold
segmentUpdate -> segUpdate, update, etc
sourceCellIdx -> srcCellIdx (in this case `source` doesn't represent a variable involving copy or linkage operations which we usually `src` and `dst` acronyms)
numNewSynapsesForSegment -> newSynapsesCount
maxSynapsesPerSegment -> maxSynsPerSeg
synapsePermanence -> perm
synapsePermanenceInitial -> symPermInit, initPerm, etc
synapsePermanenceConnected -> synPermConnected, permConnected, etc
synapsePermanenceMax -> synPermMax, permMax, etc
synapsePermanenceDecrement -> synPermDec, permDec
synapsePermanenceIncrement -> synPermInc, permInc
inferenceBacktrack -> infBacktrack
learnBacktrack -> lrnBacktrack
learnedSequenceLength -> lrnSeqLen
learnPredictedState -> lrnPredState
inferenceActiveState -> infActiveState
numIterations -> iterations, iterationNum, etc
numIterationsWithLearning -> iterationLearnNum, etc

Conventions adopted:
- Use shorten names only for well known acronyms in computational field such as `num`, `min`, `max`, `pct`, `idx`, `stats`, etc.
- Avoid shorten names for specific names in neuroscience and/or HTM-canonical: `syn`, `seg`, `lrn` (learn), `inf` (infer), `seq` (sequence), etc.

I wanted to implement this but then, what would the API look like? The syntax is `max-size [MB | GB]`. So we have two values to choose.

Choices:

* `UnloadFromSelect(max_file_size_mb=123)`, `UnloadFromSelect(max_file_size_gb=6.2)` (mutually-exclusive)
* `UnloadFromSelect(max_file_size=(123, "MB"))`, `UnloadFromSelect(max_file_size=(6.2, "GB"))` (tuple int or float with string)
* `UnloadFromSelect(max_file_size=(123, Size.MB))`, `UnloadFromSelect(max_file_size=(6.2, Size.GB))` (tuple int or float with enum)
* `UnloadFromSelect(max_file_size=123)`, `UnloadFromSelect(max_file_size=6.2 * 1024)` (only accept megabytes, auto-calculate when to show GB instead [if we even need to? Does the syntax allow for "6348 MB"?])

Have you considered having a separate git repository (e.g., "matminer_examples" or "matminer_notebooks") to store a collection of notebooks? Having them under a git repo would make them maintainable, and it is probably possible to automate testing them with new changes to matminer. A quick idea for the the parallelization: should we split it off into a separate operation? This would allow users to employ parallel featurization even if they are not using `featurize_dataframe`.

If you agree this a good idea, I implemented a version of parallel featurization a few months ago following this model: [link](https://github.com/WardLT/matminer/blob/parallel/matminer/featurizers/base.py)

It still has some problems (does not work with Python 2, requires input to be a list), but could be useful. It also does not create the `Pool` unless users request >1 thread, which has performance implications and Jupyter notebooks on Windows do not always work with Pool.

Unfortunately, we are not accepting new getters. The issue is we don't have enough maintainers on the current getters so the backlog/amount of work has gotten too large. 

All new getters would need to be implemented using napalm-yang.

We might make an exception for a getter that was implemented on all of the core platforms (Junos, EOS, IOS-XR, NX-OS, and NX-OS SSH). 

Sorry about that...but we need a better way of communicating this.

You can alternatively have your own getters that augment what we have and just subclass our classes with your class so you can use your method(s).

> Python 3 (justifiably) said that incomparable types should be incomparable rather than silently behaving in non-intuitive ways, hiding errors.

A decision was made in python 3 to stop supporting dicts with incomparable types. They aren't explicitly disallowed, but they aren't supported.

Since mixing list indexes with dictionary keys is a weird thing to do in the first place, I propose wrapping the list indexes in a dict called `_items`. Interesting idea. Thinking about the tradeoffs, there are two downsides I can see:

(1) Backticks look very similar to single-quotes. In Python in general the BDFL has pronounced that backticks won't be assigned any meaning, because of this usability problem ("syntax shouldn't look like grit on Tim's monitor"). I guess this is also somewhat of an advantage for us b/c it means that they won't be assigned any other meaning.

(2) Patsy currently relies on Python's tokenizer. Because Python doesn't use backticks as a quoting marker, the Python tokenizer crashes if fed backticks:
```
In [6]: list(patsy.tokens.python_tokenize("foo + `baz`"))
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
PatsyError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-6-024d1474cf98> in <module>()
----> 1 list(patsy.tokens.python_tokenize("foo + `baz`"))

/home/njs/.user-python3.5-64bit/lib/python3.5/site-packages/patsy/tokens.py in python_tokenize(code)
     37                 raise PatsyError("error tokenizing input "
     38                                  "(maybe an unclosed string?)",
---> 39                                  origin)
     40             if pytype == tokenize.COMMENT:
     41                 raise PatsyError("comments are not allowed", origin)

PatsyError: error tokenizing input (maybe an unclosed string?)
    foo + `baz`
         ^
```
So the only way to implement this would be to fork our own copy of the tokenizer, and then make sure to keep it up to date with each Python release. (Actually, we would need multiple forks - at least one for python 2 and one for python 3, maybe more.) Unfortunately I don't see any way to really make this viable :-(

when i pass a catalog result to expand_events that contains occurrences of an event that already ended, we need to filter out the original event in case `start is not None`.

so we end up with this:

```python

def expand_events(events, ret_mode,
                  start=None, end=None,
                  sort=None, sort_reverse=None):
    assert(ret_mode is not RET_MODE_BRAINS)
    exp_result = []
    for it in events:
        obj = hasattr(it, 'getObject') and it.getObject() or it
        if IEventRecurrence.providedBy(obj):
            occ_set = set(IRecurrenceSupport(obj).occurrences(start, end))
            if start is not None:
                if IEventAccessor(obj).start >= start:
                    occ_set.add(obj)
            occurrences = [_obj_or_acc(occ, ret_mode) for occ in occ_set]
        elif IEvent.providedBy(obj):
            occurrences = [_obj_or_acc(obj, ret_mode)]
        else:
            # No IEvent based object. Could come from a collection.
            continue
        exp_result += occurrences
    if sort:
        exp_result.sort(key=lambda x: _get_compare_attr(x, sort))
    if sort_reverse:
        exp_result.reverse()
    return exp_result
```

```python
def expand_events(events, ret_mode, start=None, end=None, sort=None, sort_reverse=None, expand_start=None, expand_end=None):
```

Lucene works by lumping segments into logical groupings called indices.  Elasticsearch extends this by calling a lucene index a "shard," and then logically groups those into "indices."  A segment is completely immutable when written.  Lucene automatically merges segments into bigger segments.  The "optimize" call is actually a forceMerge operation.

Snapshots are collections of segments, nothing more.  If a segment hasn't changed since the last snapshot, the new snapshot only points to the existing segment.  No segment which is referenced by a "newer" snapshot will be deleted when you delete an "older" snapshot.

In this sense, snapshots are not a typical backup.  You're far better off naming snapshots accordingly:

* 20 minute intervals could be named: rapid-TIMESTAMP
* hourly-TIMESTAMP
* daily-TIMESTAMP
* weekly-TIMESTAMP

Curator would handle these easily, now that you have a varied approach.

* Keep no more than 2 hours of the rapid-TIMESTAMP snapshots
* Keep no more than 12 hours of hourly-TIMESTAMP
* Keep no more than 7 days of daily-TIMESTAMP
* Keep no more than 30 days (or 28) of weekly-TIMESTAMP

In this way, you'll have an easy to read set of snapshots, recognizable by name, and an easy way to keep retention organized.  And fewer segments will be redundant at this point, as the more "merged" segments will tend to percolate up, especially if you use Curator's optimize to forceMerge segments on "older" indices for the weekly or daily snapshots.

In the general case this is impossible to fix - there's no "symbolic variable that isn't returned" - the value you're branching on is null by default because it lives in the .bss section, and there's no way to know that this particular value should not be null under some circumstances. If you really want to step this branch forward, there should be somewhere you can grab the unsatisfiable successors and manually remove the unsatisfiable path predicates from the constraints list - there's a technique called under-constrained symbolic execution that does something like this, but then you're moving into the realm of static analysis.

On the other hand, I don't really think that this branch in particular should really affect angr's overall ability to analyze this binary? this is just an initialization quirk in the middle of __libc_start_main, which is pretty good at handling lots of different degenerate initialization cases. If you just let it keep running it should totally get to main and execute correctly. (as an aside, I talked to the shellphish member who solved this challenge during quals, they used angr, but not by executing from the entry point forward, they manually pointed angr at the munge function.)

Yes you're right, in that example the `expressionLib` is an error, and should either be ignored or (better) produce an error.

The behavior of current schema salad validation is that the unrecognized fields are an error unless either (a) parsing is "non-strict" or (b) the field is a fully qualified URI like `s:author` (where the `s:` prefix expands to `http://schema.org/`)

So the generated parser needs to be fixed so:

1. check that the `class` field matches
2. check that there are no invalid unqualified fields
3. store qualified metadata fields on the object in a special metadata field

WRT _Check if mapping incompatibility in ES is per major version_ Elastic seems not to introduce any API breaking changes between minor versions. Breaking changes between minor versions affect things like the Java API, Packaging, Settings, etc (see, for example, https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/5.5/breaking-changes-5.2.html), so mappings and results are probably unaffected (the most "severe" change so far was [introducing stricter boolean values](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/5.5/breaking-changes-5.2.html))  

API can (and probably will) break between major version, but for example some breaking changes from v2 to v5 Elastic introduced backwards compatibility: https://www.elastic.co/blog/strings-are-dead-long-live-strings (see section _Backwards compatibility_)

This can be accomplished with a [`system` / `script`](https://pre-commit.com/#repository-local-hooks) hook -- it is suggested to do this as a local hook

Here's an example of how this might work (note that pre-commit will *not* manage this environment so it's up to you to make it happen):

```yaml
-   repo: local
    hooks:
    -   id: pytest
        name: pytest
        entry: venv/bin/pytest
        language: script
        pass_filenames: false
        # alternatively you could `types: [python]` so it only runs when python files change
        # though tests might be invalidated if you were to say change a data file
        always_run: true
```

Also note that running tests as part of your commit might frustrate your users, generally we try and stick to _fast_ hooks (linters) when possible.

We'll talk this week, but my recollection from last year was either no one understood how to write a grammar for ds9, or we tried and found it incompatible.

With a functional parser like the one we've started on, we can guarantee 100% coverage given enough programmer time.  I don't know if the same is true for a grammar-based approach; it's not obvious to me whether ds9 regions follow a single self-consistent grammar.

Probably the existing code could be made more clear by breaking it down into individual region types; right now, there are extra `if` statements around for the cases that have indefinite numbers of parameters (e.g., polygons, annuli).  I won't object to a BNF-based approach if it can be demonstrated, but I have to see it to believe it.

I may have gone overboard with the `sorted` calls, my goal was to make them explicit to developers, but you're right that they're not really needed for lists (assuming they're inserted into the lists in a repeatable fashion). Plus I didn't consider that some developers might expect them to be in some specific unsorted order.

So one example I've seen this for is pyopenssl, which has:

```
extras_require={
    "test": [
        "flaky",
        "pretend",
        "pytest>=3.0.1",
    ],
    "docs": [
        "sphinx",
        "sphinx_rtd_theme",
    ]
},
```

Then I think these `extras_require` items get added to `provides_extras` [here (dist.py#L416)](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/a0723a66bf7950ee470971ac9931d751a7dd76f3/setuptools/dist.py#L416).

I have been compiling with Python 3.6, which I would think should have these in a fixed order, but that doesn't seem to be happening. Plus it would be nice if it worked on older versions of Python as well.

What do you think if we sort the order [here (dist.py#L410)](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/a0723a66bf7950ee470971ac9931d751a7dd76f3/setuptools/dist.py#L410) instead? Then we can remove all the other `sorted` calls except for maybe the `project_urls.items()` which may be unordered.

I understand if these reasons are not as compelling to you as they are to me.

Okay so it turns out there's a workaround/undocumented feature specifically for license files. This can be included in `setup.cfg`:

```
[metadata]
license_file = LICENSE
```

...and then the text of the license will be in the dist-info (and, it seems, the actual package as well). There's no way to do this for "other" files (e.g. README, docs etc) that I can find .. those can only go into source dists.

```python
from __future__ import division
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
from autograd.util import quick_grad_check


def some_function(x, y):
    return x + y + x*y

class A(object):
    def call(self, X):
        self.ans = np.zeros(X.shape)
        for t in range(X.shape[0]):
            self.ans = some_function(X[t], self.ans)

        return np.sum(self.ans**2)


a = A()
print grad(a.call)(np.random.randn(5))
```

The code also works if the updated value of `self.ans` gets reused in future calls to `call` instead of getting reset to zeros like in the example I wrote. That just means the function changes every time you call it, which autograd can handle but `quick_grad_check` can't (because it invokes the function multiple times to check its numerical gradient):

```python
        return np.sum(self.ans**2)
```

I agree that (4) is probably just bad. I included it for completeness, but prefer `reset()` being explicit if allowed.

I wouldn't allow both (1) and (2), I think. They're slightly different notions of what repeat iteration means -- getting cached results vs. an updated/new set of results.
Combining them seems like it raises some issues. For example, if you wanted to `reset()` after the initial call stored results, maybe you should be allowed, and that "clears the cache"? I'm not sure we want to open up those problems.

> However, I feel like many users don't read the docs until they run into a problem, so I am very in favor of option 3. We should at the very least let people know they are doing something wrong if they are looking for more results at the end of a PaginatedResource.

Glad to hear it! I also am most in favor of making it an error to iterate multiple times, as it gives us a good place for messaging.
Adding a `NOTE` in the docs is probably good too, though I agree that most people wouldn't read it.

fget_object doesn't return a Python value - it just creates a file.  But it does a stat_object() to get the object etag, then uses the etag to construct partial filenames so it can resume an aborted download.

My original suggestion was to return the result of stat_object() from fget_object(), so that when you do an fget_object(), you also get the etag of the object "for free", without having to do another stat_object().

But looking again at the code, the only reason fget_object() does a stat_object() is to get the etag to use for a filename.  Rather than do an API call for that, it makes more sense to just hash the bucket name + object name strings and use that to construct a partial download filename.  It saves a network API call.

For programs that actually want the etag for verification, like HashBackup, they can do a stat_object() and get it.

It ends up being an efficiency thing: programs that don't want etag don't do a stat_object(), fget is faster because it avoids an API call, and programs that do want etag can do one stat_object() instead of 2.

I can not reproduce your bug. Migrations seems that they work in the very limited extent that is implemented, that is described in README and that has been asked by users in any previous issue, if I remember. I will explain it more verbosely.

The attitude to migrations in Salesforce was initially: "Do nothing really, mainly don't cause problems." The issue #56 explained us that people need also fast tests without SFDC only with sqlite3 in the memory. This was the main purpose of migrations. If you remember Django 1.7, migrations were in about ten times slower there than in Django 1.8 or newer. Upgrading any project with Salesforce migrations from Django 1.7 to Django 1.8 was also problem under some circumstances. I have not heard yet that someone really uses other database for SalesforceModel, except for fast tests. We decided later not to allow to create migration files. It was OK until Django 1.9 where no table is created in alternative databases for SalesforceModel, though everything works with sqlite3 in tests. As I explained, I'm not sure that it is really desirable, maybe after cutting support for Django 1.7 or so. I expected that other databases would work permanently as a not yet required bonus, fortunately I have never claimed it explicitly in the documentation.

Fair enough for the home page, which could be a link to the project as a whole and updating it is valid.

But doing so for the icons page, and especially specific icons, effectively breaks **every** existing link to those pages. Because anyone linking to the icons page or a specific icon of `.io` *must* be expecting 4.7, and none of the 5.0 icon classes will work on 4.7.

This is not beneficial for anyone - old projects now have broken links, and you now likely have people with no clue what's going on getting redirected to a broken icon list. Because, to 4.7 users, the 5.0 icon list is broken. You might be hoping this encourages people to upgrade - but the people visiting these links likely have no control over the version they are using and won't have a clue why it's not working.

We've already updated the links we're aware of in our projects. But I'm concerned about other projects, including at least one large framework, that now have broken links that are not feasible to update yet trivially fixed here by adjusting a redirect.

Sure, leave the home page redirecting to the new one. But, please, at least preserve links to the old content via redirect of the `/icon` path and subpaths.

Hi @kmonachopoulos ,
The depth frame native format Z16 stands for [16bit/unsigned/little endian](https://01.org/linuxgraphics/gfx-docs/drm/media/uapi/v4l/pixfmt-z16.html).
To render it with grayscale levels you need to convert it to a similar type OpenCV matrix type, probably [CV_16UC1](http://ninghang.blogspot.co.il/2012/11/list-of-mat-type-in-opencv.html)

In the demos the depth frame is often converted into RGB space to enhance visualization traits:  
 16bit grayscale->24bit RGB (8bit per channel).   
In these cases the `CV_8UC3` would be the appropriate conversion type, like in the [following example](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/wrappers/opencv/imshow/rs-imshow.cpp#L24).

This issue has been open for nearly 2 years and still the credentials section is gravely under documented: https://github.com/aws/chalice#credentials

Amazon should know that not providing a clear policy document/walk-through is an excellent way for accounts to be exploited, by those users who "just want to get something up and working", they inevitably end up providing dangerous amounts of access to their IAM users, and never go back to fix those permissions.

I shudder to think how many people are using a CLI user with AdministratorAccess to deploy their chalice apps.

You can use `@xray_recorder.capture()`  to annotate `PynamoDB` call. The decorator will automatically create a top level subsegment and record whatever exception thrown from `PynamoDB` client. See https://github.com/aws/aws-xray-sdk-python/blob/master/aws_xray_sdk/core/recorder.py#L317 which does the heavy lifting for the decorator.

This is not ideal since the stack trace is not in the same subsegment as the actual http status code, API parameters, endpoint etc. But it will at least be present on the parent of the actual call subsegment. 

But this also makes sense as the `PynamoDB` client call reflects a logical operation that might not tied to any individual outgoing http request. In another word, the exception thrown by the client is determined by the aggregated results from lower level details.

Please let me know your thoughts.

If you are concerned about messing with other things the user is doing plotting wise I suggest taking `ax` as an optional input and then doing

```python
if ax is None:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
```

That way if the user wants to use this in conjunction with other plotting they can pass in an axes to put it on (and if it messes with what ever else is there, that is their problem) or if the user does not pass in an `Axes` makes a new figure which will not (directly) mess with the user (it will do so _indirectly_ if the user is running other code that use `plt.plot` and such to plot to the current axes as the current axes will change via `plt.subplots()`.  If you are paranoid you can set the the current axes back to what it was before

```python
if ax is None:
    old_ax = plt.gca()  # which make create a figure 
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    plt.sca(old_ax)
```
but that runs the risk of creating two figures where you only needed one, but such are the risks of using global state).

Hey, shy friend here, I'm sure my use-case(s) are pretty atypical and specific so I'll try to explain. 

We have a code base for our robots, simulations ML-stuff and other tools which used to be almost entirely in C, but things are being added in C++. All this stuff needs to compile and link on a couple different platforms including some real time OSes. We keep certain compiled libraries centrally and then link against them at compile time, so that these are stable, debugged and no surprises when they're running the bigger robots.

In the past it's been GCC across the board (and still is for the most part), but for a number of reasons we're trying to move everything to clang. Now as far as I know, linking C libraries compiled in GCC or clang should really be fine, but not so much for C++. However, we really don't want any surprises when it comes to real time / robot control stuff so everything should be compiled and linked with the same toolchain (for each OS).

Anyway, for now my dirty hack updating the path works to use conda python when I need it and I keep the generic python compiled with clang for the tools I'm building in python that'll be used with the robots. But it seems that clang will only become more popular since it's default on Mac and there's some nice API and low level stuff that will catch on with IDEs I imagine. A little example - if you're using the YouCompleteMe engine for semantic code completion it crashes immediately when the python it finds is complied with gcc.

(Disclaimer: I might be totally mistaken about something - but from tinkering around for a few days I think it makes sense)

@msarahan in case you did not see yet, I created a solution for this:

https://anaconda.org/gqmelo/conda-wrappers

This basically uses another tool I created ([exec-wrappers](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/exec-wrappers)) to create wrappers for the environment it is being installed.

So if I install it on an environment called ```test``` I get a python wrapper on ```~/miniconda/envs/test/bin/wrappers/conda/python``` which I can use as if it were the real python interpreter.

I've found out an anaconda documentation explaining [how to use an IDE](https://docs.continuum.io/anaconda/ide_integration) with anaconda, but basically tells to point to the python interpreter, which does not cover cases when an activate is needed. So I think this is a very important feature to have a good integration with IDE's.

What do you think about something similar becoming a built-in feature? I was thinking about conda creating such wrappers whenever an environment is created/updated.

Yep, that's it

> Activating an environment prepends that environment's directories to PATH. Thus activating two environments at the same time in the same terminal session is somewhat ambigous. Which environment should have precedence? Since we can't be ambigous about what environment is active, one environment can be active in a session at a time.

Precedence would depend on the order or activation, ie each new env gets its path prepended to PATH. That's how it works currently on our cluster with simple bash scripts, and it works not so bad for most of the softs we use.

> Each user should be able to activate a conda environment for each terminal session they have open. Each terminal session has its own shell environment. For example, on my linux system, if I want to activate two conda environments, I need to open two terminal sessions. Since each terminal session has its own shell environment, I can activate a different conda environment in each session without any problems. There are no conda environments the need to be deactivated.

Hum, yes, no problem in having multiple shells with specific activated env. Our use case is that user want to run as a job a big pipeline that uses many different software, that are womewhat unrelated. A script like this:
```

#!/bin/bash
source activate softA
source activate softB
source activate softC
source activate softD

my_huge_pipeline.sh|py|pl|... --that uses --internally the -4 softs
```

> When using preinstalled environments, it might be a good idea to ensure somehow that users don't change the preinstalled conda environment. One problem that can arise is when a user upgrades packages that breaks another users's code. This is one of the several problems that conda environments solve very well.

I *think* it's not a big problem if we don't give write permissions to users for the system-wide conda, and let them create their own environments (if they need to) in their /home:

```
conda create -p /home/my_env softA softB
source activate /home/my_env
```

I had a look around the code, but nothing stuck out to me as an easy way of improving performance. The problem (from my extremely quick and poorly informed glance!) is that you end up resolving every single value, which includes going through any middleware and having to coordinate promises. Keeping that functionality while being competitive with just spitting out dicts directly seems rather tricky.

[The proof of concept I've got](https://github.com/mwilliamson/python-graphjoiner) sidesteps the issue somewhat by parsing the GraphQL query, and then relying on the object types being able to generate the requested data directly, without having to further resolve values. Assuming the approach is sane, then it's hard to see how to reconcile that approach with the normal GraphQL resolve approach.

Hiya, @py-in-the-sky. The touch points are really just in two places. 

For wrapping NDB's Future type, you have to connect their Future to the Deferred object that `graphql-core` uses internally. It should be as easy as checking when the Resolver returns a NDB Future & then chaining the callback to call `deferred.errback()` or `deferred.callback()` with the result or exception. 

The other end of the chain is optionally overriding the `execution_result` to convert the Deferred back into a NDB Future. 

The middleware should probably be about 30-40 lines, and fairly straightforward. 

Unfortunately I don't have too much time right now to devote to this project. But if you want to send over some code I'd be more than happy to review. 

As far as tests go. https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/master/tests_py35/core_execution/test_asyncio_executor.py is comprehensive enough and there's probably a fairly 1 to 1 equivalent for using NDB's Future type.

I've found the answer. TLDR: use a [`MiddlewareMediator`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/middleware.py#L10).

The entrypoint for graphql queries is the [`execute` method](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executor.py#L31)

The relevant bit is this conditional:
```python
    if middleware:
        if not isinstance(middleware, MiddlewareManager):
            middleware = MiddlewareManager(*middleware)
```

So what happens when you don't supply a `MiddlewareManager`, but choose to supply a middleware callable (a function or a class instance with a `resolve` method)?

Well everything operates normally for a while. First, [`execute_operation`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executor.py#L64) ... [occurs](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executor.py#L88).

Assuming you've passed `allow_subscriptions` to the `execute`, `schema.execute`, or just used `subscribe`, then [`execute_operation`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executor.py#L108) calls [`subscribe_fields`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executor.py#L162).

Inevitably, [`subscribe_fields`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executor.py#L182) calls [`subscribe_field`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executor.py#L251) which is where the trouble occurs.

[In this function](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executor.py#L290), a result is needed, so first, your resolve function is grabbed from the `MiddlewareMediator` - the one you didn't supply with `wrap_in_promise=False` as a kwarg. So your resolve function will now be returned as a promise.

So next up, [`resolve_or_error`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executor.py#L309) is called, which ultimately results in the `executor` (by default, a [`SyncExecutor`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executors/sync.py#L1), but if you've passed a custom one, that's OK).

As you can see, `executor.execute` actually does the calling of your `resolve` function. But, alas, the function it's calling returns the subscription-unfriendly (damned?) promise and [`subscribe_field` barfs](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/6df8a6312b579a6a1454bcf29a566ce5d0fa9849/graphql/execution/executor.py#L296).

So the moral of the story is to supply a `MiddlewareMediator` as such:
```python
from graphql.execution.middleware import MiddlewareMediator

my_middleware_mediator = MiddlewareMediator(my_middleware1, mymiddleware2, wrap_in_promise=False)

schema.execute(statement, middleware=my_middleware_mediator, allow_subscriptions=True)
```

Good luck out there.

Take a look at https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.handlers.html#logging.handlers.SysLogHandler.emit

In python 3.3 and above, it's possible to use the ident attribute to identify the origin of a message, but there's no proper way to do it in earlier versions. Even then, the ident string is just prepended to the message, which is what I did with the syslog formatter.

Without that change, websockify's messages in syslog stand out for being the only ones without anything to identify where they came from.

I'll try to keep my commit messages more succint.

You're almost there. You fixed one issue and created another: The input to the embedding layer must be integer (so `x = T.imatrix()` is correct), but the mask will be multiplied by a float32 tensor in the recurrent layer, so if you set the mask to int as well, the result will be upcast to float64:
```
>>> import theano
Using gpu device 0: GeForce GT 640 (CNMeM is disabled)
>>> a = theano.tensor.scalar()
>>> b = theano.tensor.iscalar()
>>> a.dtype
'float32'
>>> b.dtype
'int32'
>>> (a*b).dtype
'float64'
```
Solution: Just set the mask to be a `T.matrix()`, and provide `x_train_mask` in dtype `int8` or `bool` (otherwise Theano will correctly complain that it cannot safely convert int32 to float32 without loss of precision).

This should be done in a completely different place - in `filter-fasta.py` (via changes to `Reads.filter`).

One argument will tell `filter` to turn this:

```
>id_4751-66_2016
ACGT
```

into

```>id_4751-66_2016[id_4751-66_2016]
ACGT
```

and a second argument will (optionally) provide a regex for the ids, in which case the first match group from the regex when applied to the sequence ids will be put in the square brackets. So If a regex was also given in the above example and it was `_(\d+-\d+)_`, the following would result:

```>id_4751-66_2016[4751-66]
ACGT
```

such a filtering step could then be run as a last step after converting to AA sequences and extracting ORFs. Each ORF would have the original sequence id at the end of its `>` line in the resulting FASTA, which would allow `proteins-to-viruses.py` to regroup them later.

Hi @pathasreedhar , 
1. You are using a linear array. At best you can locate the sources in a half-plane, that is you can find the azimuth up to a symmetry with respect to the axis of the array.
2. You are operating in 3D space (by providing 3D coordinates).

I am not sure how FRIDA handles linear array. But MUSIC or SRP-PHAT should give you something at least. Both of them operate a grid search to find the most likely locations. You can solve the two above problems by:
1. Reduce the dimensionality to 2D by providing 2D coordinates for your array
2. You need to provide a list of candidate locations for the DOA. By default, the azimuth location are 0 to 359 in 1 degree increments. You should change this to 0 to 180 degrees. The relevant parameter is `azimuth` (see the doc [here](http://pyroomacoustics.readthedocs.io/en/pypi-release/pyroomacoustics.doa.music.html#module-pyroomacoustics.doa.music)).
3. You should try to plot the spatial spectrum to inspect it and check if you can see the three peaks corresponding to the sources.

Note that both SRP-PHAT and MUSIC work by estimating the spatial spectrum and then find `num_src` peaks in the estimated locations.

Hi @Granitosaurus ! :wave: :)

Been thinking about this, I don't like just passing along in this case, though I'm not sure if I can articulate precisely why, but let me try.
For starters, the methods receiving the arguments are different abstractions, so this leads me thinking that it will make maintenance quite more complex. Like, we don't have any control of which arguments may be added in the future to the `tostring` method. Their behavior may depend too much on the lxml version being used.
So I'd prefer not to make the same arguments that method take as part of the Parsel API simply passing them through.

I'd be in favor of either passing along explicitly an argument that we're interested (`format=True` for pretty printing) or adding a new dict argument `lxml_format_options` for the extra kwargs to be passed to `tostring`, which we can then document that are passed to `lxml.etree.tostring`.

This way, if in the future Parsel gets another backend or something (as suggested in #29), the API will be consistent.

Another reason a direct passthrough isn't a good idea is that it would allow to override the values being set by Parsel, which doesn't look that great -- is this needed in some use case?
If it's really needed, than we should make the argument name to be `lxml_format_options_overrides` to make explicit they're overriding what Parsel thinks it's true. :)

About the `_tostring_method` being redundant (as in having same value as `self.type`), however they are two different concepts and it makes sense to have them decoupled (for example, in the future we can ).

Does this make sense?

It's not a unicode issue, it's just that @elpimous work is defining a new way of handling chars and that the `native_client/deepspeech.cc` decoding code is not aware of it. His alphabet is:
```
# Enter here your personal alphabet, without any punctuation-space. See alphabet_readme.txt #
a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h,i,j,k,l,m,n,o,p,q,r,s,t,u,v,w,x,y,z,é,è
```

And the code https://github.com/elpimous/alfred_robot/blob/master/Deepspeech/util/alphabet_converter.py#L40 basically builds an array of chars. So it is close to what we do with ASCII computation. Since `deepspeech.cc` has no knowledge of that, and because his chars 'é' is next to 'z' in his alphabet, when the model decodes what should be a 'é' it indeed has the same ASCII code as '{'.

I know that in Linux, they catch parity errors by prefixing them with `\xff\x00`
A normal `\xff` byte is prefixed with another `\xff`
So a `\x42` byte with a parity error will be read as `\xff\x00\x42` and a `\xff` byte without parity error as `\xff\xff`
I can't check right now what an `\xff` byte with parity error would look like, but it should be either `\xff\x00\xff` or `\xff\x00\xff\xff`
To me that would be quite manageable to deal with. Again, this is only for Linux, so I agree it would be difficult to come up with an implementation that satisfies every platform. But couldn't we make it an optional setting that users can set at their own risk? Something like 
`ser = serial.Serial(port='/dev/ttyUSB0', parity='S', parity_error='linux')` 
I would be willing to implement something like that, because I need it myself anyway to trick my device into reading 9 bits. 
Let me know what you think.

nice!

pluggable serialization will be a lot simpler if you just put serialize/deserialize in isolated methods, rather than bundling them in with dispatch.

There are two choices for serialization:

1. (what you chose) serialize directly to bytes.  This is obviously the easiest and most pluggable for serializing simple small data.
2. serialize to *lists* of bytes.  This is less pluggable, because it ~always requires subclasses to define their own function, rather than trivially providing a library function.

The first is easiest to write, and easiest to drop in new serialization, but only the second can give you zero-copy, because a zero-copy message invariably turns an object into two or more parts (metadata + buffer).

As for callback/errback, I don't think passing an Exception to the callback makes sense, so you should probably specify an errback.  Unfortunately, the signature you provided makes optionally specifying the errback impossible.  You *could* define a single errback at the class level.  I'm not sure how best to deal with that.

Sorry, but this is way to vague and unspecific to help you.

From what I can see you try to override the widget on a Model and not on a Form which is not the way how things work. (https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/topics/forms/modelforms/#overriding-the-default-fields)

I had similar problems, that I solved using two steps:

First, use `ModelForm` for the form, and include the `cropping` field. In addition, as documented, specify the `ImageCropWidget` in the widgets option of the meta class._

```
from django import forms
from image_cropping import ImageCropWidget

class FooForm(forms.ModelForm):
    class Meta:
        widgets = {
            'image': ImageCropWidget,
        }

        model = Foo
        fields = ('image', 'cropping')
```

Second, make sure that you include the `cropping` field when displaying your form (if you include the entire form in your template using `{{ form }}` then you should be fine.


Question to the author of this repo: could you show us how to achieve this with a normal `forms.Form` instead of `forms.ModelForm`? I'm curious to see which `Field` members I would have to add to my form in this case.

@robertnishihara 

This makes sense logically; however from a scheduling perspective, it would be convenient (perhaps essential) to check the status of the lock itself.

### Current
```
import ray, time
@ray.remote(num_gpus=1)
class GPU(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

actor1 = GPU.remote()
time.sleep(int)
del actor1
```

### Proposed
```
import ray, time
@ray.remote(num_gpus=1)
class GPU(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

actor1 = GPU.remote()
actor1.gpu_status()
actor1.delete()
```

My defense for this request:
1. Ray should be considered a one-stop shop for scheduling
2. User defined lock checks aren't a bad thing, but it isn't obvious from the consumer perspective that ray -can't- naturally provide this feedback.
3. GPU is a special resource, in the fact that it doesn't handle attempted over-saturation like CPU bound ops.
4. In most Machine Learning applications (and at least in mine), I need to have explicit control over every GPU. The idea that I could do this was what initially attracted me to Ray.
5. Lots of modern GPU scheduling libraries provide this feedback.

@ericl 

`is the idea that you'd use gpu status to make scheduling decisions` : Yes, this is what we would like. 
`there are many actor processes sharing a set of GPUs?` : Yes. We have multiple libraries contending for GPU resources based on their business purpose. Sharding the GPU memory is essential.

#### Our Workflow:
- A numpy array is placed into the ray cluster

- If the inference algorithm determines the numpy array needs to be inferred:
  - Based on the in-the-moment resource availability, we schedule a GPU inference
- If the inference algorithm determines the numpy array needs to be stored:
  - We move the numpy array to the non-production cluster, execute a transform (non-GPU) and store the array in cold storage.

Later, when batch ML training is performed, we pull from the cold store and place into the non-production cluster. This array becomes part of a larger training job depending on the library (TensorFlow, PyTorch, etc) that is running the distributed job through ray.

`tensorflow basically never release[s] ... GPU resources` : Yes, it is an issue:
- With PyTorch, we have to `del cudaObject;torch.cuda.empty_cache()`
- With TensorFlow, we have to `session.close()` or similar

`Would it be useful to be able to allocate fractional GPU resources to processes? This could allow for more fine-grained multiplexing of processes.` : Yep, this would totally rock.

`wouldn't it be required to create an actor and have it get scheduled on a particular type of GPU` : We're growing into AWS and need to spin up and terminate GPU instances based on unanticipated load spikes. While we would know which instances we pre-allocate for this action (and subsequently their GPU specs) it is much more convenient to query the cluster in-the-moment and retrieve granular details about the available cluster's capabilities.
You should mention that they need to create their own `gcp-project` (see the example-full.yaml), as it doesn't work right out of the box...

The above example looks like this, if considered of only the first two time slices.
![example](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8504351/11900053/5a57a8cc-a5c9-11e5-8a97-ed2f32d5b57d.png)
The nodes `a_0` and `c_0` are the nodes which don't have an intra-connection(i.e. connection among the nodes inside the time slice that they belong to). These are nodes that provide a constant noise to the next time slice. 
 The problem is that for every `intra-connection` or `inter-connection` in the 0th timeslice, it is replicated for the same in the 1st timeslice in the method `initialize_initial_state`. The nodes having a constant noise aren't taken care of, and thus the bug.
The rectification is fairly simple inside the method of `DynamicBayesianNetwork`. But the problem arises when you consider the same for inference inside `dbn_inference.py`. I suggest you work out the details accordingly. Let me know if you have any further doubts.

```python
    def train(self, data, batchSize=1, dropout=0.0):
        random.shuffle(data)

        loss = 0
        for batch in self.batch(data, batchSize):
            dy.renew_cg()
            errors = []
            for (i, o) in batch:
                errors.append(self.forward(i, dropout, o))

            error = dy.esum(errors)
            loss += error.value()
            error.backward()
            self.trainer.update()

        return float(loss) / len(data)
```

My accuracy method:
```python
    def predict(self, data):
        return [self.forward(i) for i, o in data]

    def accuracy(self, data):
        good = total = 0.0
        predicted = self.predict(data)
        golds = [o for (i, o) in data]

        for pred, gold in zip(predicted, golds):  # prediction, gold vectors
            for p, g in zip(pred, gold):
                if g == 0:  # 0s are fake, so ignore them
                    continue
                total += 1
                if p == g:
                    good += 1

        return good / total
```

Some (late, sorry) comments on this issue:

- In the RNNs, it is essential for the transduce / add_inputs to be on the state, because of the flexibility to transduce from arbitrary states, and not just the initial one. It is also needed for incremental cases (i.e. truncated BPTT). 

- In the bi-rnn builder, someone added this functionality on the builder without me noticing, causing an inconsistency with the LSTM interface. In retrospect, I am not really opposed to this, especially as I do not see a way to make the analogue case (transduce on the state) for the bi-rnn, as its not clear what the analogue of state is.

- The current PR suggests adding the functionality *also* to the LSTMBuilder. The upside is the LSTM and BiLSTM builders can be used interchangeably in some cases. The downside is redundancy in the API that can lead to confusions. I tend against this API change, but not strongly so, and am open to being convinced otherwise.

- In any case, the functionality should be implemented in the base RNN class and not the specific builders.

There's a bit of nuance: There are two ways of timing out:
 - During execution. This will cause an interrupt to be requested.
 - Waiting for output. This will cause a halt of the kernel. All subsequent cells will be explicitly failed (an exception is raised at the start of `runtest`.

After a requested interrupt, we should get the traceback from the interrupt, so the second condition should not be reached in cell 2. Therefore, cell 3 should not be explicitly failed, and the kernel should be available. It should therefore succeed (it is after all a simple print statement).

The test you added is better than the one that was there before, because it captures both sides of the if-condition. However, both it and the previously existing test are trying to test *too much*.  It is testing the internals of the auth client, which means you are mocking them out, which is why the previous test didn’t raise an error.  We shouldn’t be messing with the internal member variables of the GAuthClient, or worse yet with those variables internal member variables (as was the case with setting `auth_client.creds.token`), in a unit test for the AIOConnection.  Because we tried to mock those out, we inadvertently added those variables to auth_client, so an AttributeError wasn’t raised. 

Probably part of the reason this happened is because the separation of logic between AIOConnection and GAuthClient isn’t clean.  I think the best fix would be to move the entire `set_valid_token` method to the GAuthClient, because the http client shouldn’t have to care about tokens or expiry.  There should be only one public method like `auth_client.authorize()` or `auth_client.assert_authorized()` (or whatever) and then the auth client itself decides whether a new token needs to be created.  In addition to a better separation of logic, when the method is tested it will be along with the other auth client unit tests, which should be using a real object, and it's much less likely we'll accidentally add attributes.
