Hmm, I'll bet this is related to a language setting. It could possibly be related to LibreOffice, but I would have expected to hear about it before now if so.

The basic idea is that Word stores most styles by the name you give them, which is the name they show up with in the Word UI. For some reason though, the heading styles are an exception to this. I expect it is related to a historical naming in a prior feature so this exception was needed for backward compatibility or something.

Anyway, the only way we've known to identify these so far is by specific name, in particular these 'Heading {n}' names and 'Caption'. If the originating platform doesn't respect this, or perhaps shouldn't use those particular names because its language is not English, then that would explain this behavior.

I think I would identify the root cause and see if I could solve it there. If not, I think I'd change the style names in the XML to be consistent with the standard so all the remaining processing could be done with straight `python-docx`.

I've just experienced this problem from a docker perspective. I use nginx and certbot containers, yet I may want to perform a scripted openssl check against the cert in cron to determine if I need to update the certs (no point increasing load on lets encrypt) on the host system. 

Upon this check completing, I would then run the certbot container and orchestrate the reloading of nginx configuration in the nginx container. I'm unaware of anything that can do this autonomously with certbot currently, although in hindsight it would kind of make sense that the certbot client should be able to perform a localised expiration check before checking for a renewal. 

I digress; this results in the problem my script trying to resolve the symlink to the archive folder which references the full paths as used in the docker container (currently the default path of configuration) which, due to some physical hardware restriction and volume encryption choices, mean that the symlinks don't resolve to the correct location on the host file system i.e. `/etc/letsencrypt` instead of `/encryptedvolume/etc/letsencrypt`.

I can *gaffa tape* this by using a single soft directory symlink on the host system to point `/etc/letsencrypt` at the right place, but I just thought I'd mention it as scenario is affected by this design choice for those in container land doing silly things.

By the way, it looks like you are rebasing your commit and pushing it fresh each time. That is good practice in some repos, but what we do here is keep all our commits separate, even if they are small or we aren't sure about them. Once the PR is ready, we merge it with a squash commit, so all those tiny commits get squashed into one. The advantage of this approach is that when you push individual commits I can see the diffs versus the last version of the PR I looked at.
Also, we need to modify the `MANIFEST.in` file for whichever package ends up containing the dhparams so it is included in the shipped packages.

So, oddly enough, we will probably have to make nginx a second-class plugin to make it a first-class plugin.

That is, certbot-auto currently auto-installs nginx and apache. We need it to not do that, because then we change the workflow of `run` from automatically choosing apache to asking the user to interactively select between apache and nginx.

So, doing this will require:

- delete `hidden = True`
- update website instructions to recommend plugin
- change from Alpha to Beta
- have certbot-auto not install nginx

from highcharts import Highmap
import highcharts.highmaps.highmap_helper as highmap_helper
import geopandas as gpd
import json
from shapely.geometry import Polygon

def custom_geojson():
    chart = Highmap()
    dummy = gpd.GeoDataFrame(

            [

TBH, I'm inclined to backport

* [ ] ``dict,{} --> collections.OrderedDict``
* IOMemoryOrdered, OrderedIOMemory
* os.environ[RDFLIBORDEREDDICT]

RDFLib has no limit as such, the limit is your patience :D

You don't say what store you use, I assume memory?

RDFLib isn't really build for speed, I would say that 6M triples in the
memory store should be fine, but it always depends on the queries you run
(and you latency requirements).

That query will do the cartesian product of all actors and all writers and
find the properties between them, that is quite a few triples we check for.

If you want better performance I use Jena Fuseki wrapped in a
SPARQLUpdateStore.

Ah yes! This is a python 2 vs 3 issue. The numpy that gets linked to ParaView must be compiled on python 2.7 which is why I recommend nestling PVGeo in its own python 2.7 conda env. I've been careful to make sure PVGeo can be compiled on any version of Python which is why its still works for ParaView but when you link numpy, that'll crash it! 

If you'd like to use all of PVGeo's filters that depend on numpy v1.10+, you will need to create a conda env for python 2.7 to hold PVGeo and a ParaView compatible numpy.

From your anaconda prompt:
```sh
$ conda create -n PVGeoEnv python=2.7

# Install VTK through conda
$ conda install -n PVGeoEnv vtk

# activate the virtual env before installing PVGeo ( i think this syntax is correct)
$ source activate PVGeoEnv

# install pvgeo
(PVGeoEnv) $ pip install PVGeo==1.1.16
```

Then link the numpy in that virtual environment to ParaView

@messense For Python 2.7, commit https://github.com/jxtech/wechatpy/commit/3610e8112fbee2d224c11b961ec73ea3ce65e0af introduce a new problem: cache pollution in `quote()`. It causes `UnicodeDecodeError` in subsequent calls to `quote()` with a non-ascii value, if any function in wechatpy which involves in calling `quote(..., safe='')` (the `safe` parameter is a unicode) gets called first in a Python process.

I can confirm that this bug still exists in wechatpy 1.7.4.

For example, after starting a Django dev server, first make an OAuth request in WeChat devtools, then make a fuzzy search with non-ascii characters in Django admin. A typical traceback would look like this:

```
  ...
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.13/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/urllib.py", line 1297, in quote
    if not s.rstrip(safe):
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe4 in position 0: ordinal not in range(128)
```

The corresponding interactive traceback view in Django would look like this:

```
...
/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.13/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/urllib.py in quote
1290.         (quoter, safe) = _safe_quoters[cachekey]
1291.     except KeyError:
1292.         safe_map = _safe_map.copy()
1293.         safe_map.update([(c, c) for c in safe])
1294.         quoter = safe_map.__getitem__
1295.         safe = always_safe + safe
1296.         _safe_quoters[cachekey] = (quoter, safe)
1297.     if not s.rstrip(safe): ...
1298.         return s
1299.     return ''.join(map(quoter, s))
1300.
1301. def quote_plus(s, safe=''):
1302.     """Quote the query fragment of a URL; replacing ' ' with '+'"""
1303.     if ' ' in s:

Local vars
Variable | Value
      -- | --
cachekey | ('', 'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789_.-')
  quoter | <built-in method __getitem__ of dict object at 0x10e5b4d70>
       s | '\xe4\xb8\xad'
    safe | u'ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789_.-'
```

As you can see, the `safe` variable from `_safe_quoters` dict (as a cache) becomes a unicode string, which ought to be a byte string.

If you make the fuzzy search in Django admin first, everything is fine.

The proper fix in wechatpy should be calling `quote` with byte strings, like this: `quote(self.redirect_url, safe=b'')` (because of the `from __future__ import unicode_literals` statement at the top of a file).

A temporary fix in an existing (Python 2.7) project would be calling the following code in a bootstrap script, so the `quote()` cache is filled with a proper value:

```
from urllib import quote


quote('non-empty-string', safe='')
```

A good place for a Django project would be `<project_name>/__init__.py`.

Oh, interesting... this could be a big part of the issue why the expressions `__globals__` are `numpy`  functions:
```
>>> import dill
>>> from sympy import symbols, lambdify
>>> a,b,c = symbols("a b c")
>>> expr = a + b + c
>>> l_expr = lambdify([a, b, c], expr)
>>> l_expr.__module__
'numpy'
>>>
```
A workaround for functions resulting from `lamdify` might be able to be applied to `dill`, because they seem to be able to be easily identified:
```
>>> l_expr.__doc__[:22] == 'Created with lambdify.'
True
```
Although, I'm not a huge fan of adding code that's specific to 3rd-party objects in `dill`, there is precedent to do so (`numpy.ndarray` objects, for example)... and the objects in `sympy` are in the upper tier of objects that should be considered.

>Limits looks to be pretty high by default:

I'll just hardcode some client credentials for now.

>Is it really good idea to ask user for placing result in the config file? Note that in case of Google, you receive authorization code (not access token), which is one time password to get actual access token and refresh token (used later to request new access tokens). So that value is used only once and is useless later. But on the other hand, access token is changed frequently (by default expire in one hour) - this is why I've chosen to write it in some file, instead of asking the user for modifying config...

I guess that means we can ask the user to store the refresh token in the config, and fetch access token each sync?

I really want to avoid complexity here until everything else is stable.

Patches welcome :)

# Testing procedures


### Prerequisites
  - docker on your machine (mac|win use https://www.docker.com/community-edition, linux can install through pkg management)  (check run `docker ps` should return empty list, since no containers running)
  - Makefile support (windows run under cygwin||mingw)


### Setup:

```
git clone git@github.com:learningequality/kolibri.git kolibri-test-pr
cd kolibri-test-pr/
git fetch origin pull/3775/head:dockerfiles3test
git co dockerfiles3test
make dockerbuildbase
```


### Test 1: run a pex built locally

1.1: build a pex using `make dockerbuild`
1.2  edit section `dockerdemoserver` in Makefile to set `--env KOLIBRIBUILD_PEX_PATH="/kolibribuild/kolibri-0.11.0a2.dev_git.77.gea83d77.pex" \`
(replace with whatever pex name generated in the kolibribuild/ directory)
1.3 Run `make dockerdemoserver`
1.4 Check demo server runs on localhost:8080 (no setup wizard and has sample channel imported)


### Test 2: run a pex from buildkite

2.1: build a pex using `make dockerbuild`
2.2  edit section `dockerdemoserver` in Makefile to set `--env KOLIBRI_PEX_URL="https://www.googleapis.com/download/storage/v1/b/le-downloads/o/kolibri-buildkite-build-3775-6917-kolibri-0.11.0a2.dev_git.77.gea83d77.pex?generation=1529861122464070&alt=media" \`
2.3 Run `make dockerdemoserver`
2.4 Check demo server runs on http://localhost:8080 (no setup wizard and has sample channel imported)

Note: URL obtained via https://storage.googleapis.com/le-downloads/kolibri-release-6917-report.html



### Test 3: run devserver

3.1: Run `make dockerdeveserver`
3.2: wait a little for the web packs to build
3.3: Check demo server runs on http://localhost:8000 (and prompting for setup wizard)

If we decide to bundle compiled libraries and this design doesn't account for systems where these libraries don't work or collide with other system libraries, then we shut out some unknown minority of platforms from ever using Kolibri. It's very hard to work around flaws in an application that wasn't designed to co-exist with the causes of the flaws :)

Problems identified:

1. SNI (which could be a Cloudflare requirement) is missing on old platforms: We could add a dependency for the package `python3-openssl`, since it seems that if you install pyOpenSSL, it would work. @lyw07 could you try running the same commands after installing `python-openssl` ?

1. Making sure that Kolibri works without `cryptography`: Should we add an entry to the test matrix that doesn't have `cryptography` at all. Currently, this isn't the case for Morango nor Kolibri's test matrices.

1. Making sure that the bundled `cryptography` package doesn't collide with system packages: Add a version of our source dist that doesn't contain anything compiled and add `python3-cryptography` as a dependency (which is also the consequence of adding dep. `python3-openssl`). In the longer run, perhaps write a Debian package that compiles stuff for specific architectures.

@jonboiser @rtibbles Yes I think the logic I'm trying to implement could fix #2206 as well. I'm implementing `import` currently. Will post my code here soon once it's done so everyone can take a look before I open a PR.

It did trickle down, but it is also the standard name to use to describe bidirectional text related variables and properties. e.g. https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/unicode-bidi, the bidirectional isolation tag in HTML: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/bdi, https://developer.android.com/reference/java/text/Bidi.html

Happy to call it something else, but it is industry standard, not just derived from an idiosyncratic Django implementation.

Would definitely be good to abstract the i18n utils out, will leave this in this PR for now - but can do it in my broader i18n refactor.

I think I understand why this is happening.  About 15 minutes in, the persistent connection with graph.facebook.com is getting terminated.  The next time `FacebookAdsApi` makes an HTTP request using this connection, it fails with `BadStatusLine`.

To handle this, I wrap `FacebookAdsApi.call` in a retry decorator after I instantiate it:

```py
facebook_ads_api = FacebookAdsApi(facebook_session)
facebook_ads_api.call = my_retry_decorator(facebook_ads_api.call)
```

(The decorator I'm using is from [this great library](https://github.com/rholder/retrying) if anyone is interested.)

It's planned already to have a perm to read all DAGs and another to write on all DAGs. We're thinking that many environment will have "read all dags" as part of their lower level of access.

DAGs are not namespaced at the moment and namespacing DAGs is out of scope for this PR/feature. If/when namespacing/tagging is introduced, related perms and logic can be created.

DAG number in organizations is usually in the 10s or hundreds, larger environments are probably low thousands. Technically this is not an issue for the perm-related models.

If we want to enable per-DAG access, we need to create a set of permissions per DAG, there's just no way around it.

It's also possible to generate roles programmatically if you wanted to have regex or a rule based approach.

We could, though I don't think it's necessary yet, allow the creation of special regex-DAG-perms.
